questions.prompt: Color Pascal's triangle black for odd, white for even. What fractal pattern emerges?
hints: The shapes match. Look at the upper triangle.
questions.explanation: Pascal mod 2 produces the Sierpinski triangle.

questions.promptTex: \text{Use Lucas's theorem (mod 2) on } \binom{7}{3}. \\ 7 = 111_2, \, 3 = 011_2. \text{ Is } \binom{7}{3} \text{ odd or even?}
hints: Lucas mod 2: $\binom{n}{k}$ is odd iff every 1-bit of $k$ is matched by a 1-bit of $n$.
$3$'s bits ($011$) are all matched by $7$'s ($111$). ODD.
questions.explanation: $7 = 111_2$, $3 = 011_2$. Every 1-bit of $3$ is a 1-bit of $7$, so $\binom{7}{3}$ is odd. (Check: $\binom{7}{3} = 35$, odd. ✓)

steps.text: **Approach.** Lucas mod 2: $\binom{n}{k}$ is odd iff every 1-bit of $k$ is matched by a 1-bit of $n$.
$3$'s bits ($011$) are all matched by $7$'s ($111$). ODD.
**Solution.** $7 = 111_2$, $3 = 011_2$. Every 1-bit of $3$ is a 1-bit of $7$, so $\binom{7}{3}$ is odd. (Check: $\binom{7}{3} = 35$, odd. ✓)

questions.promptTex: \text{How many entries in row 4 of Pascal's triangle are ODD?} \\ \text{(Row 4: } 1, 4, 6, 4, 1)
questions.explanation: Only $1$ and $1$ are odd. Total: 2 odd entries.

questions.promptTex: \text{Row 4 of Pascal has 5 entries; only 2 are odd. Compute the ratio odd/total.} \\ \text{Round to 2 decimals.}

questions.prompt: What's the connection between Pascal's recurrence and XOR?
choices: Pascal mod 2 has $\binom{n}{k} \equiv \binom{n-1}{k-1} + \binom{n-1}{k} \pmod 2$, which IS XOR.
XOR is only used for binary numbers.
Pascal's triangle has no relation to XOR.
questions.explanation: Addition mod 2 IS XOR. So Pascal's triangle mod 2 = output of XOR cellular automaton starting from a single 1.

questions.prompt: If you color Pascal's triangle by entries mod 3 instead of mod 2, what kind of pattern emerges?
choices: A different fractal with self-similar structure.
hints: Mod $p$ for each prime gives a different self-similar pattern.
questions.explanation: Coloring by mod $p$ for any prime $p$ gives a self-similar fractal pattern. Different primes give different fractals.

questions.prompt: Which best describes the deep meaning of 'Pascal mod 2 = Sierpinski'?
choices: Two different definitions (recursive triangle vs. coloring binomial coefficients) describe the SAME mathematical object.
It works only for the first few rows.
The Sierpinski triangle was built from Pascal's triangle historically.
questions.explanation: It's a deep example of how very different definitions can describe the same underlying object — a recurring theme in math.
